name: Tick Bite (Lyme Prophylaxis)

key_questions: Geographic risk: known Lyme-endemic area (southern/eastern Ontario [Long Point, Turkey Point, Rouge Valley, Thousand Islands, Kingston area, Niagara, eastern ON], NS, NB, southern QC, southern MB)?
Tick species: black-legged tick (deer tick, Ixodes scapularis) — the vector for Lyme in Canada?
Tick attachment time: ≥36h attached = significant transmission risk (must be engorged)
Time since tick removal: <72h = prophylaxis window
Ability to identify tick (black-legged vs dog tick)? Submit to eTick.ca for identification (free Canadian resource)
red_flags: Bull's eye rash (erythema migrans, ≥5 cm expanding) → early Lyme disease — requires treatment not just prophylaxis
Flu-like symptoms (fever, fatigue, myalgia, headache, neck stiffness) after tick bite → treat for Lyme
Neurological symptoms or cardiac symptoms (palpitations, syncope) → disseminated Lyme — urgent refer
Rash at any other body site after tick bite in endemic area
ddx: Rocky Mountain spotted fever (rare in Canada)
Local allergic reaction to tick bite

first_line.name: Doxycycline Prophylaxis (single dose)
first_line.drugs: Doxycycline 200 mg single oral dose (adults) | 4.4 mg/kg single dose (children of all ages, max 200 mg)
first_line.notes: Indication: all 4 criteria must be met — (1) black-legged tick (Ixodes scapularis) confirmed; (2) attached ≥36h; (3) in endemic area (check PHO Lyme disease risk map — updated annually); (4) prophylaxis started within 72h of tick removal. Efficacy: 87% reduction in Lyme disease if criteria met. PEDIATRIC UPDATE (IDSA/AAN/ACR 2020; AAP): Doxycycline single-dose prophylaxis is now recommended for all ages including children <8 years — no clinically significant dental staining occurs at this single dose. Age ≥8 restriction is outdated. Dose: 4.4 mg/kg (max 200 mg) for all children. COUNSELLING: Take with a full glass of water; remain upright for at least 30 minutes after taking; can take with food to reduce esophageal irritation. Advise photosensitivity — use sunscreen, avoid prolonged sun exposure. PREGNANCY: Doxycycline is generally avoided throughout pregnancy in Canada; pregnancy = refer to physician (not pharmacist-managed under MA). ALLERGY: Doxycycline is the ONLY recommended prophylactic agent per PHO algorithm. Doxycycline/tetracycline allergy = refer to physician (no alternative prophylaxis). PRIMARY ONTARIO REFERENCE: PHO Pharmacist Assessment & Prescribing Algorithm for Lyme Disease Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (2023).
first_line.preferred_when: All 4 prophylaxis criteria met (tick species + attachment time + endemic area + within 72h)

second_line.name: Watchful Waiting (if criteria not fully met)
second_line.drugs: No prophylaxis — monitor for symptoms × 30 days
second_line.notes: If prophylaxis criteria not all met (dog tick, <36h attached, non-endemic area): observe only. Advise patient to return immediately if erythema migrans rash or flu-like symptoms develop within 30 days.

non_pharm: Proper tick removal: fine-tipped tweezers, grasp close to skin surface, pull upward steadily — no twisting, no petroleum jelly/fire
Submit tick for identification at eTick.ca (free Canadian resource for tick photos and species identification)
Save the tick (sealed container or tape to paper) for identification if eTick.ca not accessible
Report tick bite to public health if in endemic area
Prevention: light-coloured clothing, long pants/sleeves in wooded areas, DEET insect repellent (20–30%), check for ticks after outdoor activity
Monitor for symptoms (erythema migrans, flu-like illness) for up to 30 days after bite
refer_when: Erythema migrans rash (expanding bull's eye, ≥5 cm) — requires treatment, not just prophylaxis
Flu-like illness (fever, myalgia, headache) after tick bite in endemic area
Neurological or cardiac symptoms after tick bite
Pregnancy — doxycycline avoided throughout pregnancy; refer to physician
Doxycycline/tetracycline allergy — no alternative prophylaxis; refer to physician
Prophylaxis criteria not fully met but clinical uncertainty warrants physician input

references: Ontario College of Pharmacists. Minor Ailments: Tick Bite / Lyme Disease Prophylaxis. OCP Prescribing Standards, O. Reg. 256/24 (2025).
Public Health Ontario (PHO). Lyme Disease: Prevention and Clinical Resources (2024). Ontario Agency for Health Protection and Promotion.
Lantos PM, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines by IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis and Treatment of Lyme Disease. Clin Infect Dis. 2021;72(1):e1–e48.
NICE. Lyme disease: NICE guideline [NG95]. National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (2018, updated 2023).
